records.ety: [AS. nama; akin to D. naam, OS. & OHG. namo, G. name, Icel. nafn, for namn, Dan. navn, Sw. namn, Goth. namō, L. nomen (perh. influenced by noscere, gnoscere, to learn to know), Gr. 'o`mona, Scr. nāman. √267. Cf. Anonymous, Ignominy, Misnomer, Nominal, Noun.]
usage: -- Name, Appellation, Title, Denomination. Name is generic, denoting that combination of sounds or letters by which a person or thing is known and distinguished. Appellation, although sometimes put for name simply, denotes, more properly, a descriptive term (called also agnomen or cognomen), used by way of marking some individual peculiarity or characteristic; as, Charles the Bold, Philip the Stammerer. A title is a term employed to point out one's rank, office, etc.; as, the Duke of Bedford, Paul the Apostle, etc. Denomination is to particular bodies what appellation is to individuals; thus, the church of Christ is divided into different denominations, as Congregationalists, Episcopalians, Presbyterians, etc.

defs: The title by which any person or thing is known or designated; a distinctive specific appellation, whether of an individual or a class.
quotes: Whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof.
What's in a name? That which we call a rose By any other name would smell as sweet.

defs: A descriptive or qualifying appellation given to a person or thing, on account of a character or acts.
quotes: His name shall be called Wonderful, Counsellor, The mighty God, The everlasting Father, The Prince of Peace.

defs: Reputed character; reputation, good or bad; estimation; fame; especially, illustrious character or fame; honorable estimation; distinction.
quotes: What men of name resort to him?
Far above . . . every name that is named, not only in this world, but also in that which is to come.
I will get me a name and honor in the kingdom.
He hath brought up an evil name upon a virgin.
The king's army . . . had left no good name behind.

defs: Those of a certain name; a race; a family.
quotes: The ministers of the republic, mortal enemies of his name, came every day to pay their feigned civilities.
They list with women each degenerate name.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Named (nāmd); p. pr. & vb. n. Naming.]

defs: To give a distinctive name or appellation to; to entitle; to denominate; to style; to call.
quotes: Thus was the building left Ridiculous, and the work Confusion named.

defs: To mention by name; to utter or publish the name of; to refer to by distinctive title; to mention.
quotes: None named thee but to praise.
Old Yew, which graspest at the stones That name the underlying dead.

defs: To designate by name or specifically for any purpose; to nominate; to specify; to appoint; as, to name a day for the wedding; to name someone as ambassador.
quotes: Whom late you have named for consul.

defs: To designate (a member) by name, as the Speaker does by way of reprimand.
